card.title: The Broken Windows Theory
card.teaser: Can a single shattered window really turn a neighborhood into a crime scene? The evidence is wilder than you'd think.
card.coverImagePrompt: A dimly lit urban alley at dusk, a single shattered tenement window with jagged glass catching the last orange light, graffiti on the brick, cinematic and moody

lessons.title: An abandoned car, two cities, one experiment
lessons.body: In 1969, psychologist Philip Zimbardo parked two identical cars — hood up, plates removed — one in the Bronx, one in Palo Alto. The Bronx car was stripped in ten minutes. The Palo Alto car sat untouched for a week. Then Zimbardo smashed its window with a sledgehammer. Within hours, locals joined in. Why did a single break turn quiet Californians into vandals?

visual.imagePrompt: A battered 1960s sedan on an empty suburban street, one window freshly smashed, scattered glass glittering on the asphalt

lessons.title: The theory in one line
lessons.body: In 1982, criminologists James Q. Wilson and George Kelling argued that visible signs of disorder — broken windows, graffiti, litter — signal that nobody's watching. That invitation, they claimed, emboldens bigger crimes. Fix the little stuff, they said, and the serious stuff fades.

keyTerms.definition: Visible cues that social norms aren't being enforced — vandalism, trash, loitering
Focusing police effort on minor offenses to prevent major ones

visual.imagePrompt: A graffiti-covered subway car interior with flickering fluorescent light, 1980s aesthetic, grainy film texture

lessons.title: New York's grand experiment
lessons.body: In the 1990s, NYPD Commissioner Bill Bratton took the theory literally. Officers cracked down on subway fare-jumpers, graffiti artists, and windshield-washers — the small stuff. Over the decade, NYC's murder rate fell by roughly 70%. Supporters called it a miracle. Critics pointed out that crime was falling nationwide — in cities that never embraced the theory at all.

visual.imagePrompt: NYPD officers in 1990s uniforms walking a graffiti-scrubbed subway platform, steam rising, gritty urban realism

lessons.title: What the research actually shows
lessons.body: A clever 2008 Dutch study by Kees Keizer found that people were twice as likely to litter or steal in an alley strewn with graffiti than in a clean one. Powerful evidence that disorder is contagious. But sociologist Robert Sampson's Chicago research found that what really predicts crime isn't broken windows — it's concentrated poverty and weak community ties. The windows, he argues, are a symptom, not the cause.
lessons.funFact: The Dutch experiment involved a fake envelope with a visible €5 note poking out of a mailbox. Near graffiti, 27% of passersby stole it. Near a clean mailbox, only 13% did.

visual.imagePrompt: A side-by-side split image: pristine Amsterdam alley on left, same alley covered in graffiti on right, overhead perspective

lessons.title: The uncomfortable legacy
lessons.body: Broken windows reshaped cities — but it also became the intellectual foundation for 'stop-and-frisk,' which disproportionately targeted Black and Latino New Yorkers and was ruled unconstitutional in 2013. The theory's lesson is double-edged: small cues really do shape behavior, but deciding what counts as 'disorder' — and who gets policed for it — is a deeply political choice.

quiz.question: Who introduced the Broken Windows Theory in their 1982 Atlantic article?
options: James Q. Wilson and George Kelling
Robert Sampson and William Julius Wilson
quiz.explanation: Wilson and Kelling published 'Broken Windows' in The Atlantic Monthly in March 1982.

quiz.question: What did the 2008 Dutch study by Kees Keizer demonstrate?
options: Graffiti has no effect on behavior
People litter and steal more in disordered environments
quiz.explanation: The experiment showed measurable jumps in littering and theft near graffiti — evidence that visible disorder does influence behavior.

quiz.question: Which major criticism challenges the theory's role in 1990s NYC?
options: Crime actually rose during that period
Crime fell nationwide, even in cities that didn't use the theory
It only worked in the Bronx
quiz.explanation: Cities like San Diego saw similar drops without order-maintenance policing, suggesting broader forces were at work.

sources.title: Broken Windows: The Police and Neighborhood Safety
